item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1'd as soon as the ghost made the point about Prozac; I immediately felt dumb that I've never thought of that before. (Which is a good sign. Truly insightful things are often obvious in retrospect.). Great story.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably my favorite GAW article. A beautifully sweet act in an otherwise depressing/horrifying/etc world. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: +1, I ship it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Social media ghost girl.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: okay the ghost literally talks almost exactly like one of the friends i play d&d with so i read the whole interview in her voice which was really weird. although you could say that means the dialogue felt really realistic lmao. anyway i liked this a lot, +1.

item.title: +100 for the trans ghost
item.content:encoded: yay

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually thought a lot of the possibility while reading GAW. Given how lenient the Foundation is with them with good reason, I can imagine a researcher becoming at least a member of their chat without leaving the Foundation. Dr Prasad seems just the guy.

item.title: Re: post ghost is here the post ghost is now the post ghost is in there standing at the concession plotting her oppression
item.content:encoded: it's definitely a terepy: the movie reference

item.title: Re: post ghost is here the post ghost is now the post ghost is in there standing at the concession plotting her oppression
item.content:encoded:
"SCP-2726-A: as it turns out ghostland does not have prozac so. still sad. i'm working on it."
Was that a fucking "Terepy: The Movie" Reference
Goddamn it you can't appeal to my ancient homestuck meme knowledge it's just not fair. Now I have to upvote it! Shit!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This article in particular helped me piece together GAW a little bit more conclusively and I think I'm starting to like them as a GoI. I didn't really care one way or the other before, but this helped add some much-needed character besides "MLG memelords who hate Wondertainment and AWCY".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr. Prasad: Oh. I think I've heard of them, actually.

SCP-2726-A: yeah i thought so.

they say hi btw and they want me to tell you "ur a binch"

Dr. Prasad: I'm flattered.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Actually, I agree cww - there are probably better story lines for the Guy Who Kinda Gets It, interacting both with GAW and people on his side who kinda don't get it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Dr Prasad is not half bad either - future GAW member?

That's not the direction I would take him (he's more interesting to me as The Guy Who Kinda Gets It But Is Committed To The Other Side), but it's plausible enough that I'm curious to see how someone else would write it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Completely agree with Roget. Was all set to downvote until I got to the end of the interview and realised that I quite liked hanging around and listening to post ghost. Dr Prasad is not half bad either - future GAW member?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
where are you seeing said pop culture and self-referential humor in this article?

Black Mirror, Banksy, "clickbait" articles, creepypasta, and I'm fairly sure I saw a few popular Homestuck quotes in there somewhere, as well.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: In-universe Star Trek DS9 reference and in-universe stylistic choice, respectively.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm pretty sure Jadzia is a dimunitive of Jadwiga.

Would be weird to list someone's legal name as Johnny Blackboxes.

I Wish A Green Asshole Would Break Into My Ghost House And Shoot A Mouth Laser At Me Double-Killing Me Instantly

I'm p sure that the "i wish character X would Y and Z, killing me instantly" meme is always lowercase and it does not make sense to format it as Uppercase Imitating Newspaper Headline especially for a character who generally talks in lowercase anyway.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a few other pop culture references actually, but they're basically easter eggs that don't really matter for the article. The self-referential humor is absent as far as I can tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Probably in that there's no pop culture reference save for a discussion about Black Mirror (which serves anyway to flesh out a character), and nearly no self-referential humor; hence, where are you seeing said pop culture and self-referential humor in this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Lol yeah I have the right article. I guess even "the least" amount that it applies still doesn't do it for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really understand the question, I'm afraid. It isn't really clear to me what you are asking. ):

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: How does this apply to this article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I like Black Mirror :c

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This is one of like two GAW articles this applies to the least. Right article?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Usually I am all about ghosts of any kind, but this isn't doing it for me. Like the other articles surrounding this group of interest, any depth it may have had gets lost in the overwhelming presence of self-referential humor and pop culture references. I do not believe those are enough to carry this. -1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This skip especially succeeds because it isn't concerned with following the originally established GAW formula (memes, memes, and more memes). Instead, it's a hilarious character study with a brilliantly realized character.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Still liking GAW. This was intriguing on a number of levels. The SCP itself made me think of a technological tulpa, with a sense of humor.

At least I *think* that's a sense of humor.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
what the fuck

+1

deliver more of this good stuff directly to my face please and thank you

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: First time I've upvoted a GAW SCP. It's a good character study, but the upvote was mostly because of the tirade of Black Mirror. I hate that show.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well,well, this got complimented by Kalinin.

On the other hand, the corn god cameo, the enjoyable chat and this:

the internet ruined the internet so we don't get to have nice things any more.

I can't upvote enough.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I actually agree with this. I think that GAW's sense of humor has been established in what we've written, we should definitely move to showing the Good that they try to bring to the world, in "humorous" ways and not. And Scans really got it here.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This hits a note of sincerity and genuine character that I don't really get when reading other GAW pieces. The addendum ghost speaks in a manner that I would expect in those other pieces, but something about the way the conversation is structured allows the fact that a real person is present (or ghost-person, what have you) to come through. The fact that the phenomenon and its implications are allowed to come through naturally, rather than by heavy, artificial exposition, is another strong point. That's especially surprising given the interview format, which in so many other articles is a vehicle for a heavy-handed infodump.

Overall, a nice use of the SCP format to tell a story and do some character establishment and development.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very heartwarming.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm so happy you wrote the first good deed scp from GAW (that wasn't political in nature), and the fact that they did it for a Polish trans girl really sets the stage for things I wanna start throwing into the canon. Once again, scans, I'm lovin' it. -1.

not but seriously +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think you mean ~80.2726% of the time? Or is this a running count of how many times people have skipped over the "mashedpork" posts? And if it is a percentage, how have they pinned the number down to so many significant digits?

Actually, what happened is that I listed this as "SCP-%" in my sandbox since it's shorter to type than "SCP-XXXX", and when I used Find+Replace to change it to 2726, it picked up the % in "80%". So basically I'm a dumbass.

The Description has also been cut down as much as I think I can afford to; I'll leave the containment procedures alone for now, since I think (almost) every line of it is necessary to actually do containment properly.

And the idea of a Foundation doctor being familiar enough with Internet culture to immediately respond to the coy roleplaying *newspaper whapwhap* doesn't seem right. The moment would play over better if he reacted like your parents would.

Immediately, certainly not. But when it's your job to talk to an SCP and get information out of it, they will A) only assign you if you have some familiarity with how the internet works, and B) after a while you'll learn to get on its level regardless. This isn't the first interview between the two — I tried to establish that they're comfortable around each other at this point.

Glad you enjoyed the piece, and thanks for your feedback!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Minor thing
- Hide

> (~802726 of the time)

I think you mean ~80.2726% of the time? Or is this a running count of how many times people have skipped over the "mashedpork" posts? And if it is a percentage, how have they pinned the number down to so many significant digits?

I don't like GAW in general because I just can't take them seriously. They never seem terribly utilitarian in their goals or methods. But here, seeing them do something so… sweet as this (as sweet as this slice of Internet culture can be) was actually pretty endearing. +1

I'd recommend shortening everything but the Addendum, where the meat of the article is. The Containment Procedures especially go on longer than necessary, and you don't really need much of an exposition here. And the idea of a Foundation doctor being familiar enough with Internet culture to immediately respond to the coy roleplaying *newspaper whapwhap* doesn't seem right. The moment would play over better if he reacted like your parents would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This nails the voice of a post gost really, really well.

EDIT: And of course Digital Love is the theme song. Awwww.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I thought the description was kind of bland, but the social media ghost boy character grew on me throughout the interview. I think you could probably shorten the description since the addenda is the real sell of the article but it's not terrible as it stands. +1

item.title: post ghost is here the post ghost is now the post ghost is in there standing at the concession plotting her oppression
item.content:encoded:
thanks to everyone who reviewed this

recommended listening
